content.decision_question: Should scarce reform capital be spent on compensatory measures with low friction but unproven equity impact, or held/redirected toward structural change with higher risk but a stronger documented equity mechanism?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can in principle be generated through evaluation of TEIP-style funding impact on the disadvantaged-achievement gap, resolving whether compensatory funding produces measurable gains. It cannot settle whether it is acceptable to leave structural sorting mechanisms untouched while doing so — that is a judgment about which forms of intervention count as adequate responses to documented inequality.
content.tension: Non-structural compensatory funding (teacher incentives, curriculum, instructional time) can be enacted with minimal political and legal friction and targets a documented resourcing gap, but the evidence base is weak/unevaluated and structural mechanisms (early tracking, SES-skewed access) continue operating unabated, meaning compensatory spending may only partially offset losses rather than closing the underlying gap.
content.title: Compensatory equity versus structural equity: symptom relief or root-cause change
